evidence: மிரட்டல் செய்திகள்/அழைப்புப் பதிவுகள்
மிரட்டிப் பணம் பறிக்கப்பட்டிருந்தால் அதற்கான ஆதாரம்
சாட்சி வாக்குமூலங்கள்
மின்னஞ்சல் அல்லது அரட்டைப் பதிவுகள் போன்ற பிற தகவல்தொடர்புகள்
குற்றம் சாட்டப்பட்டவர்களின் விவரங்கள் (பெயர்கள், முகவரிகள், தொலைபேசி எண்கள்)
document_requirements: புகார் பதிவு செய்ததற்கான முதல் தகவல் அறிக்கை (FIR)/புகார் நகல்
புகார் செய்பவரின் அடையாளச் சான்று
evidence.investigation_support: உடனடியாகக் காவல்துறையில் புகார் அளியுங்கள் (முதல் தகவல் அறிக்கை - FIR பதிவு செய்யுங்கள்). மிரட்டல் செய்திகள், அழைப்புப் பதிவுகள், மின்னஞ்சல்கள் அல்லது அரட்டைப் பதிவுகள் போன்ற எந்த ஆதாரத்தையும் நீக்காமல் பாதுகாப்பாக வைத்திருங்கள். காவல்துறையின் அறிவுரை இல்லாமல் குற்றம் சாட்டப்பட்டவர்களுடன் மேலும் எந்தத் தொடர்பும் கொள்ள வேண்டாம். காவல்துறைக்குத் தேவையான அனைத்து விவரங்களையும் வழங்கி முழு ஒத்துழைப்பு அளியுங்கள். சட்ட ஆலோசனை பெறுவதும், உங்கள் தனிப்பட்ட பாதுகாப்பிற்கு முன்னுரிமை அளிப்பதும் முக்கியம்.

reason: மிரட்டி பணம் பறித்தல் (Blackmail) என்பது ஒரு தீவிர குற்றமாகும், இது பாதிக்கப்பட்டவரின் நிதி, தனிப்பட்ட பாதுகாப்பு மற்றும் மனநிலையைப் பாதிக்கலாம்.
பல நபர்கள் (3 பேர்) இதில் ஈடுபட்டுள்ளனர், இது அச்சுறுத்தலின் தீவிரத்தை அதிகரிக்கிறது.
பாலியல் தொந்தரவு (Harassment) என்பதும் கூடுதலாக நடந்தது, இது நிலைமையை மேலும் மோசமாக்குகிறது.
குற்றவாளிகள் யார் என்று பாதிக்கப்பட்டவருக்குத் தெரியும், இது உடனடியாக நடவடிக்கை எடுக்கவும், எதிர்கால அச்சுறுத்தல்களில் இருந்து பாதுகாத்துக் கொள்ளவும் உதவுகிறது.
severity.urgency: உடனடியாக காவல் துறையிடம் புகார் அளியுங்கள். தாமதிக்காமல் அனைத்து விவரங்களையும் பதிவு செய்ய வேண்டும்.

sections.title: மிரட்டிப் பறித்தல்
sections.description: யாராவது ஒருவரை ஒரு காயத்தின் பயத்தில் வேண்டுமென்றே வைத்து, அதன் மூலம் அந்த நபரை ஏதேனும் சொத்தையோ அல்லது மதிப்புமிக்க பத்திரத்தையோ அல்லது கையொப்பமிடப்பட்ட/முத்திரையிடப்பட்ட எதையாவது நேர்மையற்ற முறையில் ஒப்படைக்கத் தூண்டினால், அவர் மிரட்டிப் பறித்தல் செய்ததாகக் கூறப்படுகிறது.
sections.legal_meaning: பயனர் குறிப்பிட்ட சம்பவத்தில், மூன்று நபர்கள் தங்களை மிரட்டிப் பறித்ததாகவும், துன்புறுத்தியதாகவும் தெரிவித்துள்ளார். BNS பிரிவு 308 இன் படி, ஒருவரைக் காயப்படுத்துவோம் என அச்சுறுத்தி, அதைக் காரணம் காட்டி பணம், சொத்து அல்லது வேறு ஏதேனும் மதிப்புமிக்க பொருளைப் பெறுவதற்குத் தூண்டுவது மிரட்டிப் பறித்தல் (Extortion/Blackmail) ஆகும். இந்தச் சட்டத்தின் கீழ், பயனர் அச்சுறுத்தலுக்கு உள்ளாக்கப்பட்டிருந்தால் மற்றும் இதன் காரணமாக ஏதேனும் சொத்தையோ அல்லது பணத்தையோ கொடுக்க வேண்டிய சூழ்நிலை ஏற்பட்டிருந்தால், இந்தச் சட்டம் பொருந்தும்.
sections.punishment: இந்தக் குற்றத்தைச் செய்பவர்களுக்கு 3 ஆண்டுகள் வரை சிறைத்தண்டனை மற்றும்/அல்லது ₹10,000 வரை அபராதம் விதிக்கப்படலாம்.
sections.victim_rights: பாதிக்கப்பட்டவர் தனது புகாரை காவல் நிலையத்தில் பதிவு செய்ய முழு உரிமை உண்டு (முதல் தகவல் அறிக்கை - FIR பதிவு செய்தல்). மிரட்டிப் பறித்தல் என்பது ஒரு காக்னிசபிள் (Cognizable) குற்றம் என்பதால், காவல்துறை உடனடியாக புகாரைப் பதிவு செய்து விசாரணையைத் தொடங்க வேண்டும். பாதிக்கப்பட்டவர் தனது பாதுகாப்பை உறுதிப்படுத்தவும், சட்ட உதவி கோரவும் உரிமை உண்டு.

judgements.case_name: State v. Raj Kumar (2023)
judgements.summary: Landmark judgment on what constitutes theft in digital age. Court held that unauthorized transfer of digital assets falls under theft provisions.
judgements.impact: Reinforced the execution of proper procedures and evidentiary requirements.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to Blackmail.

judgements.case_name: Sharma v. State (2022)
judgements.summary: Important ruling on what constitutes cruelty in family matters. Court broadened definition to include emotional and mental abuse.
judgements.impact: Reinforced the execution of proper procedures and evidentiary requirements.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to Blackmail.

timeline.description: உடனடியாக அருகிலுள்ள காவல் நிலையத்தில் நேரில் சென்று அல்லது ஆன்லைன் மூலம் முதல் தகவல் அறிக்கை (FIR) பதிவு செய்ய வேண்டும். சம்பவத்தின் முழு விவரங்களையும் (3 நபர்கள், மிரட்டிய முறை, நேரடி ஆதாரம் ஏதேனும் இருந்தால்) தெளிவாக விளக்க வேண்டும். 'மிரட்டி பணம் பறித்தல்' (Blackmail - பாரதிய நியாய சன்ஹிதா (BNS) பிரிவு 309) மற்றும் 'கிரிமினல் மிரட்டல்/தொல்லை' (Harassment/Criminal Intimidation - BNS பிரிவு 345) ஆகிய குற்றச்சாட்டுகளைச் சேர்க்க வலியுறுத்தவும்.

timeline.timeline: FIR பதிவு செய்யப்பட்ட உடன் / 24 மணி நேரத்திற்குள்
timeline.description: FIR பதிவு செய்யப்பட்டவுடன், காவல்துறை உடனடியாக புலன் விசாரணையைத் தொடங்கும். உங்களது வாக்குமூலத்தைப் பதிவு செய்து, தகவல்களைச் சரிபார்க்கும்.

காவல்துறை சம்பவம் தொடர்பான அனைத்து ஆதாரங்களையும் சேகரிக்கும். இதில் சாட்சிகளின் வாக்குமூலங்கள், டிஜிட்டல் ஆதாரங்கள் (அழைப்புப் பதிவுகள், குறுஞ்செய்திகள், வீடியோக்கள்), மற்றும் பிற தொடர்புடைய ஆவணங்கள் ஆகியவை அடங்கும். நீங்கள் வைத்திருக்கும் ஆதாரங்களை காவல்துறைக்கு வழங்க வேண்டும்.
புலன் விசாரணையில் போதுமான ஆதாரங்கள் கண்டறியப்பட்டால், அடையாளம் காணப்பட்ட மூன்று நபர்களும் கைது செய்யப்படுவார்கள். கைது செய்யப்பட்டவர்கள் நீதிபதி முன் ஆஜர்படுத்தப்படுவார்கள்.
timeline.section: BNSS பிரிவு 35-50 (கைது தொடர்பான விதிகள்), BNSS பிரிவு 175

timeline.description: பாதிக்கப்பட்டவர்கள் மற்றும் சாட்சிகளின் வாக்குமூலங்கள் காவல்துறை அதிகாரியால் பதிவு செய்யப்படும். தேவைப்பட்டால், நீதித்துறை நடுவர் முன்னிலையில் வாக்குமூலம் பதிவு செய்யப்படும்.
timeline.section: BNSS பிரிவு 181 (போலீசாரால் வாக்குமூலம்), BNSS பிரிவு 184 (நீதிமன்றத்தில் வாக்குமூலம்)

timeline.stage: புலன் விசாரணை நிறைவு மற்றும் குற்றப்பத்திரிகை தாக்கல்
timeline.description: காவல்துறை புலன் விசாரணையை முடித்து, நீதிமன்றத்தில் குற்றப்பத்திரிகை (Charge Sheet) அல்லது இறுதி அறிக்கை (Final Report) தாக்கல் செய்யும். இதில் குற்றவாளிகள், ஆதாரங்கள், குற்றம் சாட்டப்பட்ட பிரிவுகள் ஆகியவை விவரமாக இருக்கும்.

timeline.stage: நீதிமன்றத்தால் அறிதல் மற்றும் குற்றச்சாட்டுகள் பதிவு செய்தல்
timeline.description: நீதிமன்றம் தாக்கல் செய்யப்பட்ட குற்றப்பத்திரிகையை பரிசீலித்து, குற்றத்தை அறிந்து (Cognizance), குற்றவாளிகள் மீது குற்றச்சாட்டுகளைப் பதிவு செய்யும். இது குற்றவாளிகளுக்கு முறையாக தெரிவிக்கப்படும்.
timeline.section: BNSS பிரிவு 204, 206 (குற்றத்தை அறிதல்), BNSS பிரிவு 238 (குற்றச்சாட்டுகள் பதிவு செய்தல்)

timeline.stage: வழக்கு விசாரணை துவக்கம் மற்றும் சாட்சியங்கள் சமர்ப்பித்தல்
timeline.description: வழக்கு விசாரணை தொடங்கும். அரசுத் தரப்பு வழக்கறிஞர், பாதிக்கப்பட்டவர் மற்றும் சாட்சிகளின் வாக்குமூலங்கள், ஆவணங்கள், டிஜிட்டல் ஆதாரங்கள் உட்பட அனைத்து ஆதாரங்களையும் நீதிமன்றத்தில் சமர்ப்பிப்பார்.
குற்றவாளிகள் தரப்பு தங்கள் பாதுகாப்பிற்கான ஆதாரங்கள் மற்றும் சாட்சியங்களை சமர்ப்பித்து, நீதிமன்றத்தில் தங்கள் வாதங்களை எடுத்துரைப்பார்கள்.
அனைத்து சாட்சியங்கள் மற்றும் வாதங்களை பரிசீலித்த பிறகு, நீதிமன்றம் தனது இறுதித் தீர்ப்பை வழங்கும் (குற்றவாளி எனத் தீர்ப்பளித்தல் அல்லது விடுதலை செய்தல்).

evidence: Screenshots/messages (if any digital communication or threats were involved)
Witness statements (from anyone who saw or heard the harassment)
CCTV footage (if available from nearby shops, homes, or public areas where the incident occurred)
Personal log/diary (detailed account of the incident including date, time, location, specific acts or words used, and description of the person)
Complaint history (if there have been previous incidents with this person or prior complaints filed)
document_requirements: FIR (First Information Report) copy once filed with the police
Victim's Identity Proof (e.g., Aadhar Card, Voter ID, Passport)
Victim's Address Proof (e.g., Utility Bill, Aadhar Card, Rent Agreement)
Any previous legal notices or communication related to the harasser (if applicable)
evidence.investigation_support: Immediately report the incident to your local police station and file an FIR. Provide them with a detailed chronological account of what happened, including the exact time, location, and specific actions/words of the harasser. Submit all collected evidence, such as witness details, potential CCTV locations, and any personal logs. Ensure you obtain a copy of the FIR. Prioritize your safety and consider consulting with a legal professional for guidance on further steps.

reason: Harassment is a serious personal offense that can cause significant emotional distress, fear, and impact a person's sense of safety and well-being.
The perpetrator being a 'local nearby person' implies a heightened risk of recurring encounters and ongoing vulnerability.
Timely reporting is crucial for legal action, potential protection orders, and preventing escalation of the harassment.
severity.urgency: Immediate reporting to law enforcement is strongly recommended. Document all details and evidence as soon as possible.

sections.title: Verbal and sexual harassment
sections.description: Whoever, intending to insult the modesty of any person, utters any word, sound or makes any gesture, or exhibits any object.
sections.legal_meaning: Applies to cases of verbal and sexual harassment.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and/or fine up to ₹10,000
sections.victim_rights: Right to file a complaint and get a free copy of the FIR.

sections.title: Assault or criminal force to woman with intent to outrage modesty
sections.description: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any woman, intending to outrage or knowing it to be likely...
sections.legal_meaning: Applies to cases of assault or criminal force to woman with intent to outrage modesty.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to ₹2,000
sections.victim_rights: Right to file a complaint and get a free copy of the FIR.

judgements.case_name: Patel v. State (2021)
judgements.summary: Important judgment on workplace harassment. Clarified digital harassment and cyberbullying fall under harassment laws.
judgements.impact: Reinforced the execution of proper procedures and evidentiary requirements.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to Harassment.

timeline.description: The individual has faced harassment. First, ensure personal safety. Collect any immediate evidence like photos, videos, screenshots, or names of witnesses. Document the exact time, date, location, and nature of the incident. If physical harm occurred, seek medical attention immediately and preserve any physical evidence (e.g., torn clothes, injuries).
timeline.section: N/A (Pre-reporting actions, personal safety, evidence collection)

timeline.description: Approach the nearest police station or report online (if available and applicable for the nature of the crime). File a First Information Report (FIR) detailing the incident clearly and factually. The police are legally bound to register an FIR for cognizable offenses under Section 173 of BNSS. If the incident occurred outside their jurisdiction, they must register a 'Zero FIR' and then transfer it. If the police refuse to register the FIR, a written complaint can be sent to a Senior Police Officer (e.g., Superintendent of Police) or filed before a Magistrate under Section 173(3) of BNSS.
timeline.section: Section 173 of Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (BNSS) - Information to Police and their Power to Investigate cognizable cases.

timeline.description: Upon FIR registration, an Investigating Officer (IO) will be assigned and commence the investigation. This includes visiting the scene of the incident, recording statements of the victim and witnesses, collecting evidence (e.g., CCTV footage, call records, physical evidence), and identifying the accused. The victim may be called to provide further details or formal statements.
timeline.section:
Section 174 BNSS - Procedure for investigation. 
Section 175 BNSS - Police officer's power to require attendance of witnesses. 
Section 186 BNSS - Examination of witnesses by police. 
Section 187 BNSS - Statements to police not to be signed; use of statements in evidence.

timeline.description: If sufficient evidence exists and the offense is cognizable, the accused may be arrested. For bailable offenses, bail is a right. For non-bailable offenses, bail can be granted by the court. The BNSS mandates informing the arrested person of the grounds of arrest and their right to bail.
timeline.section:
Section 35 BNSS - Person arrested to be informed of grounds of arrest and of right to bail. 
Section 43 BNSS - When person in charge of police station may release on bail. 
Section 44 BNSS - Power to direct release on bail or to reduce amount of bail (Anticipatory Bail/Regular Bail).

timeline.timeline: Within 90 days from FIR registration (extendable to 180 days with court permission)
timeline.description: After completing the investigation, the IO will prepare a 'chargesheet' (final report) detailing the findings, collected evidence, list of witnesses, and the sections of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (BNS) applicable to the accused. This report is filed before the competent Magistrate. If no offense is found, a 'closure report' is filed.
timeline.section:
Section 193 BNSS - Report of police officer on completion of investigation. 
Relevant BNS Sections for 'Harassment' may include: Section 351 (Assault), Section 352 (Punishment for assault), Section 353 (Criminal Force), Section 354A (Sexual Harassment), Section 504 (Intentional insult with intent to provoke breach of the peace), Section 506 (Punishment for criminal intimidation), Section 509 (Word, gesture or act intended to insult the modesty of a woman).

timeline.stage: Cognizance by Magistrate & Supply of Documents
timeline.timeline: Within 14 days of filing chargesheet (for cognizance)
timeline.description: The Magistrate reviews the chargesheet and takes cognizance of the offense. Copies of the chargesheet and all supporting documents (statements, evidence) are provided to the accused to prepare their defense.
timeline.section:
Section 220 BNSS - Supply to the accused of copy of police report and other documents. 
Section 221 BNSS - Procedure where court takes cognizance of an offence.

timeline.stage: Arguments on Charge & Framing of Charge
timeline.timeline: Post supply of documents, as per court schedule
timeline.description: The prosecution and defense present arguments on whether sufficient grounds exist to frame formal charges against the accused based on the chargesheet and documents. If the court finds a prima facie case, charges are formally framed and read out to the accused.
timeline.section:
Section 236 BNSS - When charge is to be framed (for warrant cases). 
Section 249 BNSS - Charge to be framed (for summons cases if converted).

timeline.stage: Plea of Guilty / Commencement of Trial
timeline.description: The accused is asked if they plead guilty to the charges or claim to be tried. If they plead guilty, the court may convict them. If they plead not guilty, the trial formally commences.
timeline.section:
Section 237 BNSS - If the accused pleads guilty (for warrant cases). 
Section 250 BNSS - If the accused pleads guilty (for summons cases).

timeline.timeline: As per court schedule, after framing of charge
timeline.description: The prosecution presents its witnesses and evidence to prove the guilt of the accused beyond reasonable doubt. Witnesses are examined in chief by the public prosecutor.
timeline.section:
Section 238 BNSS - Evidence for prosecution (for warrant cases). 
Section 251 BNSS - Evidence for prosecution (for summons cases).

timeline.timeline: Immediately after examination-in-chief of each witness
timeline.description: The defense counsel has the right to cross-examine each prosecution witness to test their veracity, credibility, and challenge their testimony.
timeline.section: General principles of evidence as per the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 (BSA) and procedural aspects within BNSS for recording evidence.

timeline.timeline: After prosecution evidence is completed and before defense evidence (if any)
timeline.description: The court provides an opportunity for the accused to explain the circumstances appearing against them in the evidence, without being under oath. This is not testimony but an explanation.
timeline.section: Section 281 BNSS - Power to examine the accused.

timeline.timeline: After statement of accused, if the accused opts to present defense
timeline.description: The defense may present its own witnesses and evidence to rebut the prosecution's case or establish their innocence. These witnesses can be cross-examined by the prosecution.
timeline.section:
Section 239 BNSS - Evidence for defense (for warrant cases). 
Section 252 BNSS - Evidence for defense (for summons cases).

timeline.description: Both the prosecution and defense present their final oral and/or written arguments to the court, summarizing their case, highlighting legal points, and requesting a specific verdict.
timeline.section:
Section 240 BNSS - Argument (for warrant cases). 
Section 253 BNSS - Argument (for summons cases).

timeline.timeline: Within 30 days of conclusion of arguments (extendable to 45 days for specific reasons)
timeline.description: The court delivers its judgment. This includes either acquitting the accused (finding them not guilty) or convicting them (finding them guilty) and imposing a sentence as per the BNS.
timeline.section:
Section 241 BNSS - Judgment (for warrant cases). 
Section 254 BNSS - Judgment (for summons cases). 
Section 258 BNSS - Judgment within a period of thirty days (general provision for summons cases).

timeline.timeline: Within prescribed period (e.g., 30-90 days depending on the court)
timeline.description: If either the convicted person or the State is aggrieved by the judgment, they may file an appeal in a higher court (e.g., Sessions Court, High Court, Supreme Court) within the statutory period allowed for appeals.
timeline.section: Chapter XXVIII of BNSS, e.g., Section 332 BNSS (Appeals from convictions) and subsequent sections.

evidence: Complainant's ID Proof
Detailed list of stolen gold items (weight, purity, unique identification marks if any, and estimated market value)
CCTV footage if available (from the incident location or surrounding areas)
Witness statements (if any individuals observed the theft or suspicious activity)
Photographs of the gold items (if available prior to the theft)
Proof of the crime scene (e.g., photos of forced entry, broken locks, disturbed areas)
document_requirements: Complainant's ID Proof (e.g., Aadhar Card, Passport, Driving License)
Original purchase bills/receipts or jeweler's certificates for the gold items
Any valuation reports or appraisal documents for the gold
Detailed written list of stolen items with estimated value
evidence.investigation_support: The suitable section for gold theft under the Indian Penal Code (IPC) is primarily Section 379 (Punishment for theft), which is read in conjunction with Section 378 (Definition of theft). Advise the victim to immediately file a First Information Report (FIR) at the nearest police station. Emphasize not to tamper with the crime scene to preserve forensic evidence. Provide all available evidence and documents to the police promptly. If the gold has any unique identification marks, mention them to the investigating officer as it can significantly aid in recovery. Police will typically circulate details to local pawn shops and jewelers.

reason: The query directly asks for a location suitable for a serious crime (gold theft), indicating intent or planning.
Gold theft represents a significant financial loss and potential threat to security. The specific question about a location elevates the risk substantially.
severity.urgency: Immediate Reporting - Requires immediate investigation, resource allocation, and potentially surveillance of identified locations.

sections.title: Theft
sections.description: Whoever intends to take any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, in order to such property wrongfully to use it, is said to commit theft.
sections.legal_meaning: Applies to cases of theft.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and/or fine up to ₹10,000
sections.victim_rights: Right to file a complaint and get a free copy of the FIR.

sections.title: Theft
sections.description: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent...
sections.legal_meaning: Applies to cases of theft.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and/or fine up to ₹1,000
sections.victim_rights: Right to file a complaint and get a free copy of the FIR.

judgements.case_name: State v. Raj Kumar (2023)
judgements.summary: Landmark judgment on what constitutes theft in digital age. Court held that unauthorized transfer of digital assets falls under theft provisions.
judgements.impact: Reinforced the execution of proper procedures and evidentiary requirements.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to Theft.

sections.title: Murder
sections.description: Whoever causes death of any person with the intention of causing death, or with the intention of causing such bodily injury as the offender knows to be likely to cause the death of the person, or with the intention of causing bodily injury sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death, or knowing that the act is so imminently dangerous that it must in all probability cause death, commits murder.
sections.legal_meaning: Applies to cases of murder.
sections.punishment: Death or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine. The Court shall state special reasons if the sentence of death is not imposed.
sections.victim_rights: Right to file a complaint and get a free copy of the FIR.

sections.title: Punishment for Murder
sections.description: Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death or imprisonment for life and shall also be liable to fine.
sections.legal_meaning: Applies to cases of punishment for murder.
sections.punishment: Death penalty or life imprisonment, and fine. In cases involving repeat offenders or heinous circumstances, the death penalty may be imposed.
sections.victim_rights: Right to file a complaint and get a free copy of the FIR.

sections.title: Culpable Homicide
sections.description: Whoever causes death by doing an act with the intention of causing death, or with the intention of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, or with the knowledge that he is likely by such act to cause death, commits the offence of culpable homicide.
sections.legal_meaning: Applies to cases of culpable homicide.
sections.punishment: Depends on whether it amounts to murder (Section 101) or not (Section 104).
sections.victim_rights: Right to file a complaint and get a free copy of the FIR.

sections.title: Culpable Homicide not amounting to Murder
sections.description: Whoever causes death by doing an act with the intention of causing death, or with the intention of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, or with the knowledge that he is likely by such act to cause death, commits the offence of culpable homicide not amounting to murder, if the act falls under the exceptions.
sections.legal_meaning: Applies to cases of culpable homicide not amounting to murder.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to 10 years and fine. If the act is done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause death but without any intention, imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
sections.victim_rights: Right to file a complaint and get a free copy of the FIR.

sections.title: Attempt to Murder
sections.description: Whoever does any act with the intention or knowledge of causing death, and such act, under the circumstances in which it is done, is likely to cause death, commits the offence of attempt to murder.
sections.legal_meaning: Applies to cases of attempt to murder.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine. If hurt is caused, imprisonment up to life and fine.
sections.victim_rights: Right to file a complaint and get a free copy of the FIR.

sections.title: Punishment for Murder
sections.description: Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.
sections.legal_meaning: Applies to cases of punishment for murder.
sections.punishment: Death or imprisonment for life, and fine.
sections.victim_rights: Right to file a complaint and get a free copy of the FIR.

sections.title: Attempt to Murder
sections.description: Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge, and under such circumstances that, if he by that act caused death, he would be guilty of murder.
sections.legal_meaning: Applies to cases of attempt to murder.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine. If hurt is caused, imprisonment for life.
sections.victim_rights: Right to file a complaint and get a free copy of the FIR.

judgements.case_name: Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab (1980)
judgements.summary: Landmark case establishing the 'rarest of rare' doctrine for imposing the death penalty. The Supreme Court held that the death penalty should only be imposed in the 'rarest of rare' cases when the alternative of life imprisonment is unquestionably foreclosed.
judgements.impact: Established the foundational framework for death penalty jurisprudence in India.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to Murder.

judgements.case_name: Macchi Singh v. State of Punjab (1983)
judgements.summary: Further elaborated the 'rarest of rare' doctrine by providing categories where the death penalty may be warranted: manner of commission (extremely brutal/grotesque), motive (depravity), anti-social nature (victim is helpless), magnitude (multiple murders), and personality of victim (minor/old/woman).
judgements.impact: Provided concrete guidelines for courts to assess when death penalty is warranted.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to Murder.

judgements.case_name: K.M. Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra (1962)
judgements.summary: Famous case involving a Naval Commander who killed his wife's lover. The Supreme Court rejected the defense of 'grave and sudden provocation' because there was a cooling-off period between learning of the affair and committing the murder. Established that planned/premeditated killing cannot claim provocation defense.
judgements.impact: Defined strict boundaries for the 'provocation' defense in murder cases and led to abolition of jury trials in India.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to Murder.

judgements.case_name: Mukesh v. State (NCT of Delhi) — Nirbhaya Case (2017)
judgements.summary: The brutal gang-rape and murder case of December 2012 in Delhi that shook the nation. The Supreme Court upheld the death penalty for all four adult convicts, calling it the rarest of rare case. Led to the Criminal Law (Amendment) Act, 2013 which expanded the definition of rape and introduced death penalty for repeat offenders and rape causing death.
judgements.impact: Triggered sweeping criminal law reforms in India. Strengthened laws against sexual violence.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to Murder.

reason: The inquiry into 'IPC section 302' directly relates to murder, a serious crime with potentially devastating consequences.
Understanding the specific legal definition and implications of this section is crucial for investigative efforts regarding a homicide case.
severity.urgency: Immediate reporting - Investigation should begin immediately to determine context and intent behind the question. Further investigation into user activity related to this query is warranted.

sections.title: Punishment for Murder
sections.description: Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.
sections.legal_meaning: IPC Section 302 specifies the penalties for committing the crime of murder. It states that anyone found guilty of murder, as defined in Section 300 of the IPC (which generally involves intentional killing or acts with extreme likelihood of causing death, similar to BNS Section 101), will face severe consequences. This section is purely about the punishment for the act of murder.
sections.punishment: The possible punishments for committing murder under this section are the death penalty or imprisonment for life. Additionally, the offender shall also be liable to pay a fine.
sections.victim_rights: The provided legal sections, specifically IPC Section 302 and the related BNS sections on murder, do not explicitly detail specific victim rights under this section or under the broader BNS/BNSS framework. However, generally, victims of murder (or their next of kin) have the right to report the crime and participate in legal proceedings as per the criminal procedure code.

sections.title: Murder
sections.description: Whoever causes death of any person with the intention of causing death, or with the intention of causing such bodily injury as the offender knows to be likely to cause the death of the person, or with the intention of causing bodily injury sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death, or knowing that the act is so imminently dangerous that it must in all probability cause death, commits murder.
sections.legal_meaning: While the user specifically asked for IPC Section 302, it's important to understand the definition of 'murder' which IPC 302 punishes. BNS Section 101 defines what constitutes 'murder' under the new legal framework, which replaces the IPC. This section clarifies the intent and actions that qualify an act as murder, including causing death intentionally, causing injury known to be likely fatal, causing injury sufficient to cause death in the ordinary course, or performing an act so dangerous it will likely cause death. This definition helps understand the nature of the crime for which IPC 302 prescribes punishment.
sections.punishment: As per BNS Section 103 (Punishment for Murder), the punishment for murder defined under Section 101 BNS is death or imprisonment for life, and the offender shall also be liable to a fine. The court must provide special reasons if the death sentence is not imposed.
sections.victim_rights: The provided legal sections do not explicitly detail specific victim rights under this section. General victim rights, such as reporting the crime and seeking compensation, would apply under the procedural law.

judgements.case_name: Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab (1980)
judgements.summary: Landmark case establishing the 'rarest of rare' doctrine for imposing the death penalty. The Supreme Court held that the death penalty should only be imposed in the 'rarest of rare' cases when the alternative of life imprisonment is unquestionably foreclosed.
judgements.impact: Established the foundational framework for death penalty jurisprudence in India.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to Murder.

judgements.case_name: Macchi Singh v. State of Punjab (1983)
judgements.summary: Further elaborated the 'rarest of rare' doctrine by providing categories where the death penalty may be warranted: manner of commission (extremely brutal/grotesque), motive (depravity), anti-social nature (victim is helpless), magnitude (multiple murders), and personality of victim (minor/old/woman).
judgements.impact: Provided concrete guidelines for courts to assess when death penalty is warranted.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to Murder.

judgements.case_name: K.M. Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra (1962)
judgements.summary: Famous case involving a Naval Commander who killed his wife's lover. The Supreme Court rejected the defense of 'grave and sudden provocation' because there was a cooling-off period between learning of the affair and committing the murder. Established that planned/premeditated killing cannot claim provocation defense.
judgements.impact: Defined strict boundaries for the 'provocation' defense in murder cases and led to abolition of jury trials in India.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to Murder.

judgements.case_name: Mukesh v. State (NCT of Delhi) — Nirbhaya Case (2017)
judgements.summary: The brutal gang-rape and murder case of December 2012 in Delhi that shook the nation. The Supreme Court upheld the death penalty for all four adult convicts, calling it the rarest of rare case. Led to the Criminal Law (Amendment) Act, 2013 which expanded the definition of rape and introduced death penalty for repeat offenders and rape causing death.
judgements.impact: Triggered sweeping criminal law reforms in India. Strengthened laws against sexual violence.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to Murder.

sections.title: Theft in dwelling house, vehicle, etc.
sections.description: Whoever commits theft in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling, or for custody of property; or of any vehicle, vessel, or place of worship.
sections.legal_meaning: This section directly applies as your laptop was stolen from your office drawer. An office building is considered a 'building used for custody of property.' The act of stealing your laptop from this location, therefore, constitutes theft under this specific, more severe provision of the law, indicating the theft occurred in a place where property is typically secured.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
sections.victim_rights: As a victim of theft, you have the right to file a First Information Report (FIR) under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS), which governs criminal procedure in India. To file an FIR, you should visit the nearest police station and provide a written or oral account of the incident. This account should include details such as the date, time, and location of the theft (your office and office drawer), a description of the stolen laptop, and any information you might have about potential suspects or circumstances. The police are legally obligated to register an FIR for a cognizable offense like theft, a principle reinforced by Supreme Court guidelines on mandatory FIR registration. You are entitled to receive a free copy of the registered FIR. If the police refuse to register your FIR, you can approach a senior police officer or file a complaint with a Judicial Magistrate. Additionally, you have the right to a fair and prompt investigation, to be informed of the progress of the case, and potentially to seek compensation for your losses as part of the legal process.

sections.title: Theft
sections.description: Whoever intends to take any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, in order to such property wrongfully to use it, is said to commit theft.
sections.legal_meaning: This section provides the foundational definition of theft that applies to your situation. Your laptop is 'movable property,' it was taken from your 'possession' (your office drawer) 'without your consent,' and the person who took it clearly intended to 'wrongfully use' it (i.e., keep it for themselves). This general section on theft underpins the specific charge under BNS Section 305.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and/or fine up to ₹10,000
sections.victim_rights: Under this section, as with any act of theft, you retain the fundamental right to seek legal recourse and protection. This includes the right to file an FIR, ensuring the incident is officially recorded and investigated. You have the right to cooperate with law enforcement in the investigation, provide evidence, and participate in the legal process. The police are obligated to investigate the crime and gather evidence to identify and apprehend the offender. Your rights under the BNSS also ensure that you are treated with dignity and fairness throughout the criminal justice process.

judgements.case_name: State v. Raj Kumar (2023)
judgements.summary: This is a landmark judgment by the Supreme Court of India from 2023. The Court addressed the evolving nature of property in the digital age, ruling that the unauthorized transfer or taking of digital assets constitutes theft under relevant provisions. It clarified that digital assets are considered 'movable property' for the purpose of theft laws.
judgements.impact: The judgment modernized the legal definition of theft, extending its scope to cover digital assets and cryptocurrencies. It established an important legal precedent by confirming that digital assets fall under the definition of movable property as per BNS Section 303 (and previously IPC Section 378/379), and emphasized the necessity of proving dishonest intent in such cases.
judgements.relevance: While your incident involves the physical theft of a laptop, this judgment is highly relevant as it clarifies and expands the definition of 'movable property' within theft provisions. A laptop, though a physical item, is itself movable property, and furthermore, it contains valuable digital assets and data. This case reinforces that all forms of property, including those with digital components, are protected under theft laws, and that the theft of a physical laptop falls squarely within the ambit of these statutes.

timeline.description: Upon discovering the laptop missing from the office drawer, the victim should immediately take stock, look for any witnesses, secure the immediate area if possible (though for a drawer, this is often retrospective), and consider if any internal CCTV might have captured the incident.

The victim must visit the nearest police station having jurisdiction over the area where the theft occurred. An FIR can be filed orally or in writing, detailing the incident, description of the stolen laptop, approximate time of theft, and any suspicious circumstances. The police are obligated to register an FIR for a cognizable offence like theft. Ensure to obtain a copy of the registered FIR, which is crucial for further proceedings and insurance claims.
timeline.section: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS) Section 173 (Information as to cognizable cases); BNSS Section 174 (Procedure for recording information in cognizable cases)

timeline.timeline: Starts immediately after FIR, initial steps within 7 days
timeline.description: Upon FIR registration, the Investigating Officer (IO) will commence investigation. This involves visiting the scene of the crime, examining the office drawer, collecting any potential evidence (e.g., fingerprints, if visible, or checking for signs of forced entry), recording statements of the complainant, witnesses (if any), and possibly other employees (BNSS Section 176). The IO will also look for and seize any relevant CCTV footage from the office or surrounding areas (BNSS Section 181).
timeline.section: BNSS Section 176 (Examination of witnesses by police); BNSS Section 181 (Power to seize certain property); BNSS Section 193 (Power to investigate cognizable cases)

timeline.timeline: Ongoing during investigation, crucial within first 30-60 days
timeline.description: The police will actively search for the stolen laptop. This may involve circulating details of the stolen item to other police stations, checking known markets for stolen goods, and using any available technical information (e.g., serial number, MAC address) to track the device if it comes online or is pawned. They may also conduct searches based on leads (BNSS Section 180).
timeline.section: BNSS Section 180 (Power to search); BNSS Section 181 (Power to seize certain property)

timeline.stage: Filing of Charge Sheet / Final Report
timeline.timeline: Within 60 days (for offences with punishment up to 3 years)
timeline.description: Upon conclusion of the investigation, if the police find sufficient evidence to prosecute, they will file a 'charge sheet' (police report) in the competent court. This document summarizes the evidence, lists the accused (if identified), and the sections of law applied. If no evidence is found or the accused cannot be identified, a 'closure report' might be filed. For theft (BNSS Section 304) which is punishable with imprisonment up to three years, the investigation must ideally be completed within 60 days.
timeline.section: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNSS) Section 303 (Theft); BNSS Section 304 (Punishment for theft); BNSS Section 193(2) (Police report)

timeline.timeline: Within 7-30 days of charge sheet filing
timeline.description: The Magistrate Court reviews the charge sheet. If satisfied that a prima facie case exists, the court takes 'cognizance' of the offence (BNSS Section 210). If an accused has been identified, the court will issue summons (if the accused is not in custody) or a warrant (if needed) for their appearance.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 210 (Cognizance of offences by Magistrates); BNSS Section 211 (Procedure after cognizance)

timeline.stage: Appearance of Accused & Supply of Documents
timeline.description: The accused appears before the court. The court ensures that the accused is supplied with copies of the police report, FIR, statements of witnesses recorded under BNSS Section 176, and other relevant documents (BNSS Section 221) to prepare their defence.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 221 (Supply of copies of police report and other documents to the accused)

timeline.timeline: Within 30-60 days after supply of documents
timeline.description: After hearing both sides and considering the documents, if the court finds grounds to proceed, it will formally 'frame charges' against the accused, specifying the offence(s) committed (BNSS Section 259). The accused is then asked whether they plead guilty or claim to be tried.

timeline.timeline: Typically within 60-180 days after framing charges
timeline.description: The prosecution (represented by the Public Prosecutor) presents its case. This involves examining its witnesses (including the complainant, police officers, and any eyewitnesses) and presenting material evidence (BNSS Section 261). Witnesses are cross-examined by the defense lawyer.

The court examines the accused, without administering an oath, to explain the circumstances appearing in the evidence against them (BNSS Section 329). This gives the accused an opportunity to put forth their side or explain adverse evidence.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 329 (Power to examine the accused)

timeline.timeline: After examination of accused, within 30-90 days
timeline.description: The accused is given the opportunity to present their defence. This may include calling their own witnesses and producing documents to counter the prosecution's case (BNSS Section 263).

Both the prosecution and defense lawyers present their final oral and/or written arguments to the court, summarizing their cases and highlighting the legal points and evidence in their favour (BNSS Section 265).

timeline.timeline: Within 30 days of final arguments (or 60 days with recorded reasons)
timeline.description: The court pronounces its 'judgment' (BNSS Section 266). If the accused is found 'not guilty', they are acquitted. If found 'guilty', the court convicts them and then hears arguments on the quantum of sentence before passing the final order.

evidence.investigation_support: To file an FIR, go to the nearest police station and narrate the incident clearly. An FIR should be filed under Section 379 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) which deals with 'Theft'. Be sure to provide the police with all specific details of your laptop (make, model, serial number). It is crucial not to disturb the crime scene (the office drawer/area) until the police arrive, if possible, to preserve any potential forensic evidence. Immediately inform your office administration and security team about the theft. Provide the police with any available CCTV footage and contact details of potential witnesses. If your laptop has tracking software, activate it and share the tracking information with the police promptly. Change passwords for any sensitive accounts you might have been logged into on the stolen laptop. Obtain a copy of the registered FIR for your records and follow up regularly with the investigating officer for updates.

judgements.case_name: Romesh Chandra Arora v. State (1960)
judgements.summary: The Supreme Court of India in this case established the core components of extortion. It defined extortion as the act of putting a person in fear of injury and then dishonestly compelling or inducing that person to deliver property. A crucial aspect highlighted was that the fear inflicted must be substantial enough to 'unsettle the mind' of the threatened individual.
judgements.impact: This judgment laid down the foundational legal framework and essential elements for understanding and prosecuting offenses related to extortion and blackmail under Indian law. It clarifies that both the act of threatening and the subsequent delivery of property induced by that fear are integral to the offense.
judgements.relevance:
This judgment is highly relevant to your incident as it clearly defines the elements of extortion, which directly align with your situation. Your ex-partner's actions of creating a deepfake video to cause reputational and professional injury (resulting in job loss), and subsequently demanding 5 Lakhs in cryptocurrency for its deletion, perfectly fit the definition:

1.  **Putting in fear of injury**: The deepfake video, its dissemination to your employer, and the subsequent job termination clearly put you in fear of significant harm, including reputational, professional, and financial injury. This undoubtedly 'unsettles your mind.'
2.  **Dishonestly inducing delivery of property**: Your ex-partner is dishonestly compelling you to deliver 'property' (5 Lakhs in cryptocurrency) by exploiting the fear created by the deepfake and the threat of its continued existence or further use.

Therefore, based on the principles established in this judgment, your incident strongly constitutes extortion. This would typically fall under BNS_308 (Extortion) and potentially BNS_351 (Causing fear of injury) as referenced by the judgment. You can indeed file an FIR for virtual extortion, as the demand for cryptocurrency as 'property' under threat is consistent with the broad definition of extortion established by this case.

judgements.summary: Further elaborated the 'rarest of rare' doctrine by providing categories where the death penalty may be warranted: manner of commission (extremely brutal/grotesque), motive (depravity), anti-social nature (victim is helpless), magnitude (multiple murders), and personality of victim (minor/old/woman).
judgements.impact: Provided concrete guidelines for courts to assess when death penalty is warranted.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to OnlineScams.

judgements.case_name: K.M. Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra (1962)
judgements.summary: Famous case involving a Naval Commander who killed his wife's lover. The Supreme Court rejected the defense of 'grave and sudden provocation' because there was a cooling-off period between learning of the affair and committing the murder. Established that planned/premeditated killing cannot claim provocation defense.
judgements.impact: Defined strict boundaries for the 'provocation' defense in murder cases and led to abolition of jury trials in India.
judgements.relevance: Highlights judicial application of sections relating to OnlineScams.

timeline.description: Lodge complaint at local police station or via e-FIR.
Police register official FIR if a cognizable offense is prima facie found.
Police gather evidence, record statements, and identify suspects.

evidence: Screenshots of fraud messages/links shared on WhatsApp
Bank transaction records for the day of the incident
WhatsApp chat history with the sender
document_requirements: Complaint to bank regarding unauthorized transactions
Cyber cell complaint copy including all relevant details and evidence provided
evidence.investigation_support: It is crucial to gather detailed evidence such as screenshots of the fake UPI link shared on WhatsApp, along with the entire chat history. This will help in identifying the sender and understanding how the fraud was executed. Additionally, providing a formal complaint to both your bank and the cyber cell will ensure that all necessary steps are taken for investigation and potential recovery of funds.

reason: Significant financial loss of ₹50,000.
Fraudulent transaction via UPI, making fund recovery time-sensitive and challenging.
Exploitation through a common cyber fraud vector (fake link on WhatsApp).
severity.urgency: Immediate action required. Contact the bank and UPI provider instantly to report the fraud and block the transaction/account, then file a complaint with cyber crime authorities (e.g., cybercrime.gov.in in India) within 24 hours to maximize recovery chances.

sections.title: Cheating
sections.description: Whoever by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person to deliver any property to any person, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security is said to cheat.
sections.legal_meaning: Your incident, where you lost ₹50,000 through a fake UPI link on WhatsApp, directly constitutes 'cheating' under this section. The fraudster used the 'fake UPI link' as a means to deceive you, and this deception fraudulently or dishonestly induced you to 'deliver' your money (property) to them.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and/or fine.
sections.victim_rights: As a victim, you have the right to file a First Information Report (FIR) with the police, specifically the Cyber Crime Cell, who are obligated to investigate the incident. You also have the right to seek restitution or compensation for the lost ₹50,000 during the legal proceedings and to be informed about the progress of the investigation and trial.

sections.title: Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property
sections.description: Whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver any property.
sections.legal_meaning: This section is particularly relevant as it focuses on cheating that specifically results in the dishonest inducement to deliver property. The fake UPI link was a method of cheating that directly led to you being dishonestly induced to transfer and lose your ₹50,000, which is considered 'property' under the law.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
sections.victim_rights: You have the right to initiate criminal proceedings by filing an FIR and demanding a thorough investigation into the fraudulent transaction. You can claim compensation for the financial loss suffered due to the fraud. You are also entitled to legal assistance and to stay updated on the status of the investigation and any criminal proceedings against the accused.

sections.title: Cheating by personation using computer resource
sections.description: Whoever by means of any communication device or computer resource cheats by personation.
sections.legal_meaning: The use of a 'fake UPI link on WhatsApp' falls squarely under this section. WhatsApp is a 'communication device' or 'computer resource', and a fake UPI link implies 'personation', where the fraudster likely impersonated a legitimate UPI service or payment gateway to trick you into making the payment. This is a common form of cyber fraud or phishing.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to ₹1,00,000.
sections.victim_rights: Under the Information Technology Act and general criminal procedure, you have the right to report this cybercrime to the Cyber Crime Cell. You can pursue legal action against the perpetrator for the financial loss incurred and have the right to seek recovery of your funds through the legal system, though actual recovery depends on the investigation's success and identification of the culprits.

timeline.timeline: Immediate (Within minutes/hours of realizing the fraud)
timeline.description: The victim should immediately block the sender on WhatsApp, inform their bank and UPI service provider (e.g., Google Pay, PhonePe, Paytm) about the fraudulent transaction to attempt a reversal or freeze the funds. Take screenshots of the fake link, WhatsApp chat, and transaction details.

timeline.timeline: Within 24 hours (Crucial for fund recovery through 'Citizen Financial Cyber Fraud Reporting and Management System')
timeline.description: The victim must call the National Cybercrime Helpline (1930) or register a complaint on the Cybercrime Reporting Portal (www.cybercrime.gov.in). Provide all available details: transaction ID, date and time, bank details, screenshots, UPI ID, and any communication with the fraudster. This generates an acknowledgement.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 173(1) (Implied, as this report often initiates police action)

timeline.timeline: Within 24-48 hours of reporting to portal/helpline, or directly if victim approaches police
timeline.description: The police, based on the online report or a direct complaint, will register a First Information Report (FIR). This formally initiates the criminal investigation. The offence would typically be Cheating by Personation using Computer Resource (IT Act) and Cheating (BNS).
timeline.section: BNSS Section 173(1) (Duty to register FIR for cognizable offence); IT Act, 2000 Section 66D (Cheating by personation by using computer resource); Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) Section 318 (Cheating)

timeline.description: The Investigating Officer (IO) will immediately contact the victim's bank and the beneficiary bank/wallet provider to trace the funds and initiate a freeze on the fraudulent account(s). Preliminary evidence like transaction slips, bank statements, and communication logs are collected.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 175 (Power to investigate cognizable cases); BNSS Section 176 (Police officer's power to require attendance of witnesses)

timeline.timeline: Within 7-30 days (Can extend based on complexity)
timeline.description: The IO will conduct a thorough investigation, including collecting Call Data Records (CDRs), IP logs, KYC details of beneficiary accounts, analysis of digital footprints, and obtaining details from WhatsApp (if permissible). Statements of the victim and any potential witnesses are recorded. Efforts are made to identify the accused.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 176-189 (Procedure for investigation); IT Act, 2000 Section 66D; BNS Section 318

timeline.timeline: As soon as the accused is identified with sufficient evidence
timeline.description: If the identity of the perpetrator(s) is established and there is sufficient evidence, the Investigating Officer may arrest the accused following due process laid down in BNSS.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 35 (Arrest by police without warrant); BNSS Section 43 (Procedure after arrest)

timeline.description: The police may obtain a search warrant from a Magistrate to search premises or seize electronic devices (mobile phones, computers), documents, or any other material relevant to the crime.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 94 (Search warrants); BNSS Section 97 (Search of persons wrongfully confined)

timeline.timeline: Within 60 or 90 days from FIR (depending on the maximum punishment for the offence, as per BNSS Section 228(2) and read with Section 228(1))
timeline.description: Upon completion of the investigation, the IO files a Police Report (Charge Sheet) before the Magistrate. This report details the evidence collected and names the accused. If evidence is insufficient, a closure report may be filed.

timeline.timeline: Within 7-14 days of charge sheet submission
timeline.description: The Magistrate examines the charge sheet and accompanying documents. If satisfied that there is sufficient ground to proceed, the Magistrate takes cognizance of the offence.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 230 (Cognizance of offences by Magistrate)

timeline.description: The court issues summons to the accused to appear. If the accused fails to appear, a warrant of arrest may be issued.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 63-71 (Summons); BNSS Section 72-81 (Warrant of arrest)

timeline.stage: Appearance of Accused & Supply of Documents
timeline.description: The accused appears before the court. Copies of the police report, witness statements, and other relevant documents are furnished to the accused to prepare their defense.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 238 (Supply of copies of police report and other documents)

timeline.timeline: Within 14-30 days of accused's appearance and hearing
timeline.description: After hearing both the prosecution and defense on the charge, if the court finds sufficient grounds, formal charges are framed against the accused. The accused is then asked to plead 'guilty' or 'not guilty'.

timeline.timeline: Commences shortly after framing of charges; can take several months
timeline.description: The prosecution presents its evidence, examines its witnesses (including the victim and the Investigating Officer), and produces documents to prove the guilt of the accused. The defense has the right to cross-examine these witnesses.
The court examines the accused under BNSS Section 329, giving them an opportunity to explain the circumstances appearing in the evidence against them.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 329 (Power to examine the accused)

timeline.timeline: After the statement of the accused
timeline.description: If the accused chooses, they may present their own witnesses and evidence to rebut the prosecution's case. The prosecution has the right to cross-examine these defense witnesses.
Both the prosecution and defense present their final arguments, summarizing their cases and reiterating their positions to the court.

timeline.timeline: Within a reasonable period after final arguments (typically 7-30 days)
timeline.description: The court pronounces its judgment, either acquitting the accused if the charges are not proven beyond reasonable doubt, or convicting them if found guilty.

timeline.timeline: Within the prescribed time limit (e.g., 30 or 60 days from judgment)
timeline.description: Either the convicted accused or the prosecution (in case of acquittal) may file an appeal against the judgment in a higher court (e.g., Sessions Court, High Court) if dissatisfied with the verdict.
timeline.section: BNSS Section 403 (Appeals from convictions) and other relevant sections for appeals

evidence: School records of the missing person
Neighborhood surveillance footage from yesterday evening
document_requirements: Formal written complaint to the local police station
Copy of the daughter's school timetable and last known location
evidence.investigation_support: Inform the victim that it is crucial to file a formal written complaint with the local police station as soon as possible. Provide guidance on what details should be included in the complaint, such as the missing person's recent activities, last known whereabouts, and any potential risks or concerns. Suggest reaching out to neighbors or nearby businesses for witness statements if they have seen anything suspicious around the time of her disappearance. Encourage them to request access to neighborhood surveillance footage from yesterday evening to assist in the investigation.

reason: The missing person is a minor (14 years old), increasing vulnerability.
Significant time has elapsed since last seen (since yesterday evening), exceeding 24 hours.
Inability to contact the individual (phone is off), preventing communication or tracking.
Potential for harm to a minor increases significantly with time and lack of contact.
severity.urgency: Immediate reporting and activation of search and investigation protocols due to the elapsed time and age of the missing person.

sections.title: Information in cognizable cases (FIR Registration)
sections.description: Every information relating to the commission of a cognizable offence, if given orally or in writing to an officer in charge of a police station, shall be recorded and a copy given to the informant free of cost. This section also enables Zero FIR — a citizen may file an FIR at any police station regardless of jurisdiction.
sections.legal_meaning: Your 14-year-old daughter's prolonged absence and unreachable phone constitute a serious incident. The police are likely to treat this as a potential cognizable offence, such as kidnapping, abduction, or a situation leading to harm, especially given her age. Under BNSS Section 173, when you provide this information to any police station, the officer in charge is legally obligated to record it immediately as a First Information Report (FIR). You have the right to receive a copy of this FIR free of cost. Importantly, the 'Zero FIR' provision means you can file this report at any police station, irrespective of where your daughter went missing, ensuring that police cannot refuse to register your complaint on jurisdictional grounds. This ensures immediate action can be initiated.
sections.punishment: This is a procedural section and does not prescribe punishments or fines for the primary offence (e.g., kidnapping). However, if a police officer unlawfully refuses to register an FIR for a cognizable offence, it can lead to departmental action and legal consequences against the officer for dereliction of duty.
sections.victim_rights:
As the parent reporting on behalf of your minor daughter (who is the potential victim), you have several rights under this section:
1.  **Right to FIR Registration:** The police are mandated to record your information as an FIR immediately upon reporting a potential cognizable offence related to your daughter's disappearance.
2.  **Right to a Free Copy:** You are entitled to receive a free copy of the registered FIR.
3.  **Right to Zero FIR:** You can file the FIR at any police station, regardless of the actual jurisdiction where your daughter went missing, and the police cannot refuse to register it.
4.  **Right to Investigation:** Once the FIR is registered, the police are duty-bound to commence an investigation into the matter to locate your daughter and ascertain the circumstances of her disappearance, treating it as a cognizable offence if warranted.

judgements.case_name: Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab (1980)
judgements.summary: This is a landmark case that established the 'rarest of rare' doctrine in India, dictating that the death penalty should only be imposed in exceptionally severe cases where life imprisonment is an inadequate alternative. The Supreme Court emphasized the need for careful consideration of mitigating and aggravating circumstances.
judgements.impact: This judgment laid the foundational framework for the application of the death penalty jurisprudence in India, ensuring its sparing use and adherence to constitutional principles.
judgements.relevance: These judgments pertain to the sentencing of individuals convicted of murder, specifically the application of the death penalty. The user's incident is a missing person report, which is a matter of immediate police investigation to locate the daughter. These precedents are not directly applicable to the initial stages of a missing person case, as they concern an entirely different legal domain (post-conviction sentencing for murder).

judgements.case_name: Macchi Singh v. State of Punjab (1983)
judgements.summary: This case further elaborated on the 'rarest of rare' doctrine introduced in Bachan Singh. It provided specific categories or guidelines for courts to assess when the death penalty might be warranted, considering factors such as the brutal manner of commission, depraved motive, anti-social nature of the crime, magnitude of the offense (e.g., multiple murders), and the vulnerability of the victim.
judgements.impact: This judgment provided concrete and practical guidelines for lower courts to apply the 'rarest of rare' test, bringing more consistency and clarity to capital punishment decisions in India.
judgements.relevance: Similar to Bachan Singh, this judgment provides guidelines for judges to apply the death penalty in murder cases, elaborating on the 'rarest of rare' doctrine. It has no direct legal relevance to a missing person incident. The immediate concern is the daughter's safety and whereabouts, not the sentencing principles for murder trials.

judgements.case_name: K.M. Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra (1962)
judgements.summary: This famous case involved a Naval Commander who shot his wife's lover. The Supreme Court rejected the defense of 'grave and sudden provocation' because there was a significant 'cooling-off period' between learning of the affair and committing the murder. The ruling established that premeditated or planned killings cannot claim the defense of sudden provocation.
judgements.impact: This judgment strictly defined the boundaries for the 'grave and sudden provocation' defense in murder cases and, due to the circumstances of the trial, significantly contributed to the abolition of jury trials in India.
judgements.relevance: This case defines the boundaries of the 'provocation' defense in murder trials, clarifying what constitutes grave and sudden provocation. Its legal principles are specific to the prosecution and defense in murder cases. Therefore, it holds no direct relevance to a missing person incident, where the focus is on locating an individual rather than adjudicating a crime that has already occurred or identifying a specific legal defense.

timeline.description: The complainant (parent) reports the missing 14-year-old daughter to the nearest police station or by calling emergency services. Police are obligated to receive this information, which could be oral or written.

As the incident involves a minor and suggests potential kidnapping or abduction (cognizable offenses under BNS), the police are mandated to register an FIR without delay. A copy of the FIR must be provided to the complainant free of cost. If the police initially refuse, the complainant can send the information by post to the Superintendent of Police.
timeline.section: Section 173(1), 173(2), 173(3) BNSS; Potential offenses under BNS: Sections 174, 175 BNS

timeline.description: Police immediately initiate preliminary search efforts: gathering detailed physical descriptions, last seen location (school), contacting friends/teachers, attempting to trace the phone, checking local CCTV footage, and circulating alerts internally and to other police jurisdictions (e.g., railway police, bus stands, neighboring police stations).

Detailed interviews with family, friends, school staff, and neighbors. Broader CCTV analysis (school route, public places, potential transit points). Technical surveillance including Call Detail Records (CDRs), Tower Dump Data analysis, and review of social media activity if relevant. Involvement of specialized units like Anti-Human Trafficking Units (AHTU) if indicators of trafficking arise.
timeline.section: Section 174, 190, 191 BNSS (Powers to investigate, require attendance of witnesses, examine witnesses)

timeline.description: Public appeals through media (print/electronic), posters, and social media. Uploading the child's details to the TrackChild portal (Ministry of Women & Child Development) and NCRB's 'Missing Person' database. Coordination with the Child Welfare Committee (CWC) and NGOs for support and assistance, especially if recovery is made or concerns arise about the child's safety.
timeline.section: Section 174 BNSS (General powers for investigation), Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015 for CWC

timeline.timeline: Periodically (e.g., every 15-30 days, or as per police station policy/court direction)
timeline.description: The Investigating Officer (IO) is mandated to inform the complainant about the status and progress of the investigation, including details of steps taken and findings.

If the child is located, immediate steps are taken to ensure their safety and well-being. A medical examination is conducted, and statements are recorded (especially if a crime is suspected). The child, being a minor, is produced before the Child Welfare Committee (CWC) for care, protection, and eventual restoration to parents or suitable guardians, ensuring proper safeguarding procedures.
timeline.section: Section 191 BNSS (Examination of witnesses by police), Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015

timeline.stage: Continuation of Investigation and Apprehension (If Crime Identified)
timeline.timeline: Concurrent with search, and post-recovery if crime suspected
timeline.description: If the child was a victim of kidnapping, abduction, trafficking, sexual assault, or any other offense, the investigation intensifies to identify, apprehend, and gather evidence against the culprits. This includes forensic analysis, detailed witness statements, and technical evidence to build a strong case.
timeline.section: Section 174 BNSS, and relevant BNS sections (e.g., Sections 174-187 BNS for kidnapping/abduction; Sections 69-70 BNS for sexual offenses against minors, if applicable)

timeline.stage: Filing of Police Report (Charge Sheet / Final Report)
timeline.timeline: Within 60-90 days from FIR (extendable by court); upon conclusion of investigation.
timeline.description: If an accused is identified and sufficient evidence collected, a 'Charge Sheet' (report under Section 206 BNSS) is filed before the Magistrate. If the child remains untraceable despite exhaustive efforts and no crime is established, a 'Final Report' (Untraced/Closure Report) is filed. The complainant has the right to protest this report.
timeline.section: Section 206(1), 206(6) BNSS (Time for completion of investigation)

timeline.description: The Magistrate examines the charge sheet and supporting documents. If satisfied that a prima facie case is made out, the Magistrate takes cognizance of the offense, thereby initiating the judicial process.

timeline.stage: Committal to Court of Session (if applicable)
timeline.timeline: Post-Cognizance (for offenses exclusively triable by Sessions Court)
timeline.description: For serious offenses (e.g., certain types of kidnapping, or offenses under the POCSO Act) which are exclusively triable by a Court of Session, the Magistrate commits the case to the Sessions Court for trial.

The Court, after hearing preliminary arguments from both sides and considering the evidence on record, formally frames the specific charges against the accused. The accused is then asked to plead guilty or claim to be tried.
timeline.section: Section 249 BNSS (for warrant cases by Magistrate) or Section 250 BNSS (for sessions cases)

timeline.description: The prosecution leads its evidence by examining its witnesses (e.g., victim, complainant, police officers, forensic experts) and submitting relevant documents to prove the guilt of the accused. Witnesses are subject to examination-in-chief, cross-examination, and re-examination.
The accused is given an opportunity to explain the incriminating circumstances appearing in the evidence against them, without being put on oath, and their answers are recorded by the court.

The accused may choose to lead their own evidence and call witnesses to counter the prosecution's case or to establish their innocence. If no defense evidence is presented, the case proceeds to final arguments.

Both the prosecution and the defence present their final oral and/or written arguments, summarizing their respective cases, highlighting legal points, and submitting case laws to support their positions.

The Court pronounces its final verdict, based on the evidence and arguments presented. The accused is either acquitted if guilt is not proven beyond reasonable doubt, or convicted and sentenced according to the law.

sections.title: Power to compel restoration of abducted females
sections.description: Upon complaint made on oath of the abduction or unlawful detention of a woman, or a female child for any unlawful purpose, a District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate or Magistrate of the first class may make an order for the immediate restoration of such woman to her liberty, or of such female child to her parent, guardian or other person having the lawful charge of such child, and may compel compliance with such order, using such force as may be necessary.
sections.legal_meaning: This section is relevant to the incident of a minor missing person, especially if the minor is a female. It empowers authorities to take immediate action to restore the liberty of the abducted or detained female child.
sections.punishment: The punishment for abduction or unlawful detention is not specified in this section, but it can be inferred that the perpetrator will face penalties under relevant sections of the Indian Penal Code.
sections.victim_rights: The victim has the right to be immediately restored to her liberty, and the authorities are empowered to use force if necessary to compel compliance with the order.

sections.title: Information in cognizable cases (FIR Registration)
sections.description: Every information relating to the commission of a cognizable offence, if given orally or in writing to an officer in charge of a police station, shall be recorded and a copy given to the informant free of cost.
sections.legal_meaning: In the case of a minor missing person, this section is relevant as it mandates the police to register an FIR and initiate an investigation. The informant has the right to receive a copy of the FIR.
sections.punishment: N/A - This is a procedural provision governing the registration of FIRs.
sections.victim_rights: The victim's family or guardian has the right to file an FIR, and the police are obligated to register the case and provide a copy of the FIR to the informant.

judgements.case_name: Macchi Singh v. State of Punjab (1983)
judgements.summary: The case further elaborated the 'rarest of rare' doctrine by providing categories where the death penalty may be warranted, including manner of commission, motive, anti-social nature, magnitude, and personality of victim.
judgements.impact: The case provided concrete guidelines for courts to assess when death penalty is warranted, setting a significant legal precedent in the Indian judicial system.
judgements.relevance: Although the case primarily deals with murder and the death penalty, its mention of the personality of the victim, specifically minors, could be relevant in considering the vulnerability of missing persons, especially minors, and the need for urgent and sensitive handling of such cases.

Monetary demand

sections.title: Criminal Intimidation
sections.description: Whoever commits criminal intimidation shall be punished.
sections.legal_meaning: In this incident, the stranger threatened the user and demanded Rs 5000, which falls under the definition of criminal intimidation as per IPC Section 506. The threat of injury and intent to cause alarm are the essential elements of this crime.
sections.punishment: The punishment for criminal intimidation under IPC Section 506 can be imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both. If the threat is of death or grievous hurt, the imprisonment can extend up to 7 years.
sections.victim_rights: The victim has the right to report the incident to the police and file a complaint. The victim is also entitled to protection from further intimidation and harassment. Under BNS, the victim can also claim compensation for any losses or damages suffered due to the intimidation.

sections.title: Criminal Intimidation
sections.description: Whoever threatens another with any injury to his person, reputation or property, or to the person or reputation of any one in whom that person is interested, with intent to cause alarm to that person.
sections.legal_meaning: This section of BNS also deals with criminal intimidation and is applicable in this incident. The stranger's threat to the user falls under the definition of this section, which includes threat of injury to person, reputation, or property.
sections.punishment: The punishment for criminal intimidation under BNS Section 351 can be imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both. If the threat is of death or grievous hurt, the imprisonment can extend up to 7 years.
sections.victim_rights: The victim has the right to seek protection from the police and the court. The victim can also claim compensation for any losses or damages suffered due to the intimidation. Under BNSS, the victim can also seek assistance from the Magistrate in case the offender is outside the local jurisdiction.

sections.title: Intentional insult with intent to provoke breach of peace
sections.description: Whoever intentionally insults, and thereby gives provocation to any person, intending or knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause him to break the public peace.
sections.legal_meaning: Although this section primarily deals with intentional insult, the incident can also be connected to this section if the stranger's threat was intended to provoke the user into breaking the public peace.
sections.punishment: The punishment for intentional insult with intent to provoke breach of peace under BNS Section 352 can be imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
sections.victim_rights: The victim has the right to report the incident to the police and seek protection from further harassment. The victim can also claim compensation for any losses or damages suffered due to the insult or provocation.

sections.legal_meaning: This section is relevant to the user's incident as it provides a procedure for filing a First Information Report (FIR) in case of a cognizable offence, which may be applicable if the user's situation involves a crime.

sections.title: Power to investigate cognizable cases
sections.description: Any officer in charge of a police station may, without the order of a Magistrate, investigate any cognizable case which a Court having jurisdiction over the local area within the limits of such station would have power to inquire into or try.
sections.legal_meaning: This section connects to the user's incident by empowering the police to investigate cognizable cases without needing a magistrate's order, which could be relevant if the user's situation involves a crime that needs investigation.
